**Jakarta**,[[b]](#cite_note-11) officially the **Special Region of Jakarta**, previously called the **Special Capital Region of Jakarta**,[[c]](#cite_note-12) remains the [*de facto* capital](/wiki/Capital_of_Indonesia "Capital of Indonesia") and [largest city](/wiki/List_of_Indonesian_cities_by_population "List of Indonesian cities by population") of [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and an autonomous region with administrative status equivalent to a [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Indonesia "Provinces of Indonesia"). Located on the northwest coast of [Java](/wiki/Java "Java"), the world’s most populous island, the city borders the provinces of [West Java](/wiki/West_Java "West Java") and [Banten](/wiki/Banten "Banten") and faces the [Java Sea](/wiki/Java_Sea "Java Sea") to the north. Although Jakarta covers about 661.23 square kilometres (255.30 square miles), the wider [Jakarta metropolitan area](/wiki/Jakarta_metropolitan_area "Jakarta metropolitan area")—commonly known as Greater Jakarta—is one of the [largest urban agglomerations in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities"). The city serves as Indonesia’s political, economic, and cultural centre and hosts numerous national institutions, corporate headquarters, and the secretariat of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations ([ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN")).

The area that is now Jakarta has been inhabited since at least the early centuries of the Common Era and was historically associated with the port of [Sunda Kelapa](/wiki/Sunda_Kelapa "Sunda Kelapa"), which served the [Sunda Kingdom](/wiki/Sunda_Kingdom "Sunda Kingdom"). In 1527, the settlement was renamed Jayakarta, following its capture by forces of the [Demak Sultanate](/wiki/Demak_Sultanate "Demak Sultanate"). The [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") later seized the city in 1619 and rebuilt it as [Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_Dutch_East_Indies "Batavia, Dutch East Indies"), the administrative centre of the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") for more than three centuries. After the [Japanese occupation during the Second World War](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_the_Dutch_East_Indies "Japanese occupation of the Dutch East Indies") and [Indonesia’s declaration of independence in 1945](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Indonesian_Independence "Proclamation of Indonesian Independence"), the city adopted the name Jakarta and became the national capital of the newly independent republic.

As Indonesia’s principal financial and commercial hub, Jakarta plays a central role in the country’s economy and in regional trade across Southeast Asia. The city hosts the headquarters of major Indonesian corporations, financial institutions, and the [Indonesia Stock Exchange](/wiki/Indonesia_Stock_Exchange "Indonesia Stock Exchange"), and has developed into a major centre for business, media, and international diplomacy. Rapid urbanisation since the mid-20th century has transformed Jakarta into a vast metropolitan region, attracting migrants from across the Indonesian archipelago and contributing to its position as the [country’s most populous city](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_Indonesia "List of metropolitan areas in Indonesia") and [one of the largest urban economies in the region](/wiki/List_of_ASEAN_country_subdivisions_by_GDP "List of ASEAN country subdivisions by GDP").

Jakarta is a highly diverse city with no single dominant ethnic group. Its population includes large communities of [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people"), [Betawi](/wiki/Betawi_people "Betawi people"), [Sundanese](/wiki/Sundanese_people "Sundanese people"), [Chinese Indonesians](/wiki/Chinese_Indonesians "Chinese Indonesians"), and migrants from many other regions of Indonesia. [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") is the official language and the primary means of communication, while Betawi culture reflects the historical blending of local, Chinese, Arab, and European influences that developed during the colonial period. As Indonesia’s former capital and largest metropolis, Jakarta struggles with urban challenges including traffic congestion, air pollution, flooding, and land subsidence, issues that have contributed to the national government’s decision to [relocate Indonesia’s future capital](/wiki/Capital_of_Indonesia#Relocation_to_Nusantara "Capital of Indonesia") to [Nusantara](/wiki/Nusantara_(planned_city) "Nusantara (planned city)") in [East Kalimantan](/wiki/East_Kalimantan "East Kalimantan").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The area that is now Jakarta has been known by several names throughout its history. Early references to settlements in the area appear in the [Tugu inscription](/wiki/Tugu_inscription "Tugu inscription") from the [Tarumanagara](/wiki/Tarumanagara "Tarumanagara") kingdom in the 4th century, where the name *Sundapura* is recorded. In later centuries, the harbour settlement became known as *Sunda Kelapa*, serving as the principal port of the [Sunda Kingdom](/wiki/Sunda_Kingdom "Sunda Kingdom") on the north coast of western Java.[[10]](#cite_note-names-13)[[11]](#cite_note-14)

The name *Jayakarta* was introduced in the 16th century.[[12]](#cite_note-jakarta-news-2011-15) It derives from the Sanskrit words ([Devanagari](/wiki/Devanagari "Devanagari"): जयकर्त), derived from the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") जय *jaya* (victorious),[[13]](#cite_note-16) and कृत *krta* (accomplished, acquired),[[14]](#cite_note-17) meaning “complete victory” or “victorious deed.” Early European accounts recorded the name in forms such as *Jacatra* or *Jacarta*.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECortesão199027–32-18) The name marked the political changes in the region during that period and remained in use until the early 17th century.

In 1619, the [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") renamed the city *[Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_Dutch_East_Indies "Batavia, Dutch East Indies")*, after the [Batavi](/wiki/Batavi_(Germanic_tribe) "Batavi (Germanic tribe)"), a Germanic tribe whom the Dutch regarded as their ancestral people. The name Batavia remained in official use for more than three centuries during the Dutch colonial period. During the Japanese occupation in the Second World War, the city was briefly renamed ジャカルタ特別市 *Jakaruta Tokubetsu-shi* ("Jakarta Special City"),[[10]](#cite_note-names-13) after which the Indonesian form Jakarta became the official name following Indonesian independence.[[10]](#cite_note-names-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Jakarta](/wiki/History_of_Jakarta "History of Jakarta")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Jakarta](/wiki/Timeline_of_Jakarta "Timeline of Jakarta").

### Early settlements and Sunda Kelapa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early settlements and Sunda Kelapa")]

Further information: [Sunda Kelapa](/wiki/Sunda_Kelapa "Sunda Kelapa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Replica_of_the_Luso-Sundanese_Padr%C3%A3o_Monument.jpg/250px-Replica_of_the_Luso-Sundanese_Padr%C3%A3o_Monument.jpg)](/wiki/File:Replica_of_the_Luso-Sundanese_Padr%C3%A3o_Monument.jpg)

[Luso-Sundanese *padrão*](/wiki/Luso-Sundanese_padr%C3%A3o "Luso-Sundanese padrão"), a monument with a cross of the [Order of Christ](/wiki/Military_Order_of_Christ "Military Order of Christ"), commemorating a treaty between [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") and Hindu [Sunda](/wiki/Sunda_Kingdom "Sunda Kingdom") kingdoms

The area that now forms Jakarta has been inhabited since prehistoric times. Archaeological evidence from the [Buni culture](/wiki/Buni_culture "Buni culture"), which flourished on the north coast of western Java between roughly 400 BC and 100 AD,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZahorka2007?-19) indicates early settlements in the region. By the 4th century, the area formed part of the [Sundanese](/wiki/Sundanese_people "Sundanese people") kingdom of [Tarumanagara](/wiki/Tarumanagara "Tarumanagara"), one of the earliest Hindu kingdoms in the archipelago. The [Tugu inscription](/wiki/Tugu_inscription "Tugu inscription"), discovered in present-day North Jakarta and dated to around 417 AD, records hydraulic projects undertaken by King Purnawarman, including irrigation and river management works along the Chandrabhaga and Gomati rivers near the kingdom’s capital.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHellmanThynellVoorst2018182-20)

Following the decline of Tarumanagara, the region became part of the [Kingdom of Sunda](/wiki/Sunda_Kingdom "Sunda Kingdom"). From the 7th to the early 13th centuries, the port of Sunda Kelapa developed into an important trading centre connected to regional maritime networks. Chinese records such as the *[Chu-fan-chi](/wiki/Zhu_Fan_Zhi "Zhu Fan Zhi")*, written in the early 13th century, describe the port as a prosperous trading hub under the [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya") maritime empire[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBungeVreeland19833-21)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAyatrohaédi200560-22) and note that high-quality pepper from Sunda was among its major exports. By the 14th century Sunda Kelapa had become the principal port of the Sunda Kingdom, supporting trade across Southeast Asia.

European involvement in the region began in the early 16th century when Portuguese ships from Malacca arrived in 1513 while searching for new spice routes.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeuken1999?-23) In 1522, the Sunda Kingdom [concluded an alliance](/wiki/Luso_Sundanese_padr%C3%A3o "Luso Sundanese padrão") with Portugal, granting permission for the construction of a trading post in order to counter the growing power of the [Demak Sultanate](/wiki/Demak_Sultanate "Demak Sultanate") in central Java.[[12]](#cite_note-jakarta-news-2011-15) The arrangement was short-lived, however. In 1527, forces from Demak led by the military commander [Fatahillah](/wiki/Fatahillah "Fatahillah") captured Sunda Kelapa and expelled the Portuguese. The port was renamed Jayakarta[[12]](#cite_note-jakarta-news-2011-15) and later became a possession of the [Banten Sultanate](/wiki/Banten_Sultanate "Banten Sultanate"), developing into a significant regional trading centre.

### Batavia under Dutch rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Batavia under Dutch rule")]

See also: [Batavia, Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Batavia,_Dutch_East_Indies "Batavia, Dutch East Indies") and [List of colonial buildings and structures in Jakarta](/wiki/List_of_colonial_buildings_and_structures_in_Jakarta "List of colonial buildings and structures in Jakarta")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Batavia_-_Townhall_1770.jpg/250px-Batavia_-_Townhall_1770.jpg)](/wiki/File:Batavia_-_Townhall_1770.jpg)

The City Hall of Batavia (*Stadhuis van Batavia*), the seat of the governor-general of the [VOC](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") in the late 18th century by [Johannes Rach](/wiki/Johannes_Rach "Johannes Rach") c. 1770. The building now houses the [Jakarta History Museum](/wiki/Jakarta_History_Museum "Jakarta History Museum"), [Jakarta Old Town](/wiki/Jakarta_Old_Town "Jakarta Old Town")

Conflict between Prince Jayawikarta of the Banten Sultanate and the [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company") (VOC) eventually led to the establishment of Dutch control over the city. In 1619, Dutch forces under [Jan Pieterszoon Coen](/wiki/Jan_Pieterszoon_Coen "Jan Pieterszoon Coen") captured and destroyed Jayakarta, defeating both Jayawikarta’s troops and their [English allies](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"). The Dutch subsequently established a fortified colonial city on the site and renamed it Batavia,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] which became the administrative centre of the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies").

Batavia developed into a major trading hub within the [Dutch colonial empire](/wiki/Dutch_colonial_empire "Dutch colonial empire"). Commercial activity attracted migrants from across Asia, including Chinese, Arab, and other trading communities. Rapid population growth, however, also produced social tensions. In 1740, a revolt by Chinese residents led to a [violent suppression in which thousands were killed](/wiki/1740_Batavia_massacre "1740 Batavia massacre"), after which Chinese inhabitants were relocated to the [Glodok](/wiki/Glodok "Glodok") district outside the city walls.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitton2003138–39-24) Over time, Batavia continued to expand as trade increased[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENasGrijns2000145-25) and new immigrant communities settled in the city.

During the 19th and early 20th centuries, the city expanded southward as epidemics and overcrowding in the old port district encouraged new residential developments further inland. Urban planning projects created new districts such as [Menteng](/wiki/Menteng "Menteng"), developed in the 1910s as a planned residential suburb,[[23]](#cite_note-26) while [Kebayoran Baru](/wiki/Kebayoran_Baru "Kebayoran Baru") became one of the last major residential areas constructed during the late colonial period.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitton2003138–39-24) Dutch colonial rule ended in March 1942 when [Japanese forces captured the city during the Second World War](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies_campaign "Dutch East Indies campaign") and renamed it Jakarta (Jakarta Special City (ジャカルタ特別市, *Jakaruta tokubetsu-shi*), marking the beginning of a new phase in the city’s history.

### Jakarta in independent Indonesia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Jakarta in independent Indonesia")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Indonesia_declaration_of_independence_17_August_1945.jpg/250px-Indonesia_declaration_of_independence_17_August_1945.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indonesia_declaration_of_independence_17_August_1945.jpg)

[Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") reading the [Proclamation of Indonesian Independence](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Indonesian_Independence "Proclamation of Indonesian Independence") at Jalan Pegangsaan Timur Number 56, Menteng

Following the end of the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), Indonesian nationalists [proclaimed independence](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Indonesian_Independence "Proclamation of Indonesian Independence") on 17 August 1945.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWaworoentoe2013-27) In the following month, the administration of the city was reorganised as the Jakarta National Administration. During the [Indonesian National Revolution](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Revolution "Indonesian National Revolution"), however, Indonesian republican leaders withdrew from [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II")-occupied Jakarta and established their temporary capital in [Yogyakarta](/wiki/Yogyakarta "Yogyakarta"). After the [Netherlands formally recognised Indonesian independence in December 1949](/wiki/Dutch%E2%80%93Indonesian_Round_Table_Conference "Dutch–Indonesian Round Table Conference"), Jakarta resumed its role as the national capital in 1950.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWitton2003138–39-24)

During the presidency of [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno"), Jakarta was transformed into the symbolic capital of the new nation. Preparations for the [1962 Asian Games](/wiki/1962_Asian_Games "1962 Asian Games") prompted large-scale urban development projects that reshaped the city. Major infrastructure and architectural projects included the construction of the National Monument, the Hotel Indonesia complex, new shopping centres, major boulevards such as [Jalan MH Thamrin](/wiki/Jalan_MH_Thamrin_(Jakarta) "Jalan MH Thamrin (Jakarta)")-Sudirman, and the [Senayan sports complex](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Sports_Complex "Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex"). Sukarno envisioned Jakarta as a modern international city that would represent Indonesia’s independence and national identity.[[25]](#cite_note-28)

Political tensions reached a turning point in October 1965 when [an attempted coup](/wiki/30_September_Movement "30 September Movement") resulted in the killing of six senior army generals, triggering a [nationwide anti-communist purge](/wiki/Indonesian_mass_killings_of_1965%E2%80%9366 "Indonesian mass killings of 1965–66") that killed hundreds of thousands of people, and marking [the beginning of Suharto's New Order](/wiki/Transition_to_the_New_Order "Transition to the New Order"). In 1966, Jakarta was formally designated a Special Capital Region (*Daerah Khusus Ibukota*, DKI) with administrative status equivalent to a province.[[26]](#cite_note-29)[[27]](#cite_note-Statistics-2008-30) During the governorship of [Ali Sadikin](/wiki/Ali_Sadikin "Ali Sadikin") (1966–1977), the city experienced major urban reforms,[[28]](#cite_note-31) including infrastructure improvements, the expansion of hospitals and schools, and cultural development programmes, although large-scale redevelopment projects also displaced many informal settlements.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner1997315-32)

Jakarta continued to expand rapidly during the late 20th and early 21st centuries. Foreign investment during the late New Order period fuelled a major real estate boom,[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESajor2003713–42-33) although this growth was interrupted by the [1997 Asian financial crisis](/wiki/1997_Asian_financial_crisis "1997 Asian financial crisis"), which led to political unrest and [riots in 1998](/wiki/May_1998_Indonesia_riots "May 1998 Indonesia riots") that led to the resignation of President [Suharto](/wiki/Suharto "Suharto").[[31]](#cite_note-34)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriend2003329-35)[[33]](#cite_note-36) In the period that followed, Jakarta remained the centre of Indonesia’s political life and reforms,[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriend2003?-37) including the introduction of [direct gubernatorial elections in 2007](/wiki/2007_Jakarta_gubernatorial_election "2007 Jakarta gubernatorial election").[[35]](#cite_note-38) The city has also experienced sporadic [terrorist attacks](/wiki/Terrorism_in_Indonesia "Terrorism in Indonesia") since the early 2000s. More recently, the Indonesian government announced plans to relocate the national capital to [Nusantara](/wiki/Nusantara "Nusantara"),[[36]](#cite_note-kalimantan-39)[[37]](#cite_note-40)[[38]](#cite_note-41) although Jakarta continues to function as the country’s primary political and economic centre.[[39]](#cite_note-42)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Greater Jakarta](/wiki/Greater_Jakarta "Greater Jakarta")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Pantai_Ancol_-_panoramio_%281%29.jpg/250px-Pantai_Ancol_-_panoramio_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pantai_Ancol_-_panoramio_(1).jpg)

[Ancol](/wiki/Ancol "Ancol") beach in the [Jakarta Bay](/wiki/Jakarta_Bay "Jakarta Bay")

Jakarta covers 661.23 square kilometres (255.30 sq mi), making it the smallest [province in Indonesia](/wiki/Provinces_of_Indonesia "Provinces of Indonesia") by land area. However, the wider [Jakarta metropolitan area](/wiki/Jakarta_metropolitan_area "Jakarta metropolitan area") covers about 6,392 square kilometres and extends into the neighbouring provinces of West Java and Banten.[[40]](#cite_note-43) The metropolitan area includes the regencies of [Bekasi](/wiki/Bekasi_Regency "Bekasi Regency"), [Tangerang](/wiki/Tangerang_Regency "Tangerang Regency"), and [Bogor](/wiki/Bogor_Regency "Bogor Regency"), as well as the cities of [Bogor](/wiki/Bogor "Bogor"), [Depok](/wiki/Depok "Depok"), [Bekasi](/wiki/Bekasi "Bekasi"), [Tangerang](/wiki/Tangerang "Tangerang"), and [South Tangerang](/wiki/South_Tangerang "South Tangerang").

The city lies on the northwestern coast of Java at the mouth of the [Ciliwung River](/wiki/Ciliwung_River "Ciliwung River") on the [Jakarta Bay](/wiki/Jakarta_Bay "Jakarta Bay"), an inlet of the Java Sea. The northern part of Jakarta consists largely of low-lying coastal plains, some areas of which are below sea level[[41]](#cite_note-44) and therefore prone to flooding, while the southern districts are slightly higher and more hilly. Administratively, Jakarta also includes the [Thousand Islands](/wiki/Thousand_Islands_(Indonesia) "Thousand Islands (Indonesia)") archipelago located in the Jakarta Bay to the north of the mainland city.

Jakarta is situated on a flat [alluvial plain](/wiki/Alluvial_plain "Alluvial plain") with an average elevation of about 8 metres (26 feet) above sea level. Historically, much of the area consisted of swampy land, and parts of the city have been built on reclaimed coastal flats.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurrayClemensPhinnPossingham2014267–72-45) Thirteen rivers flow through Jakarta from the [Puncak](/wiki/Puncak "Puncak") highlands in the south towards the [Java Sea](/wiki/Java_Sea "Java Sea"), including the [Ciliwung](/wiki/Ciliwung_River "Ciliwung River"), [Angke](/wiki/Angke_River "Angke River"), [Sunter](/wiki/Sunter_River "Sunter River"), and [Grogol rivers](/wiki/Grogol_River "Grogol River").[[43]](#cite_note-46)[[44]](#cite_note-47) These waterways, combined with heavy seasonal rainfall and drainage challenges, contribute to the city’s recurring [flooding problems](/wiki/Flooding_in_Jakarta "Flooding in Jakarta").

Jakarta is also grappling with environmental challenges related to land subsidence and water management. Parts of the city—particularly in northern coastal areas—have been sinking by several centimetres per year, largely due to excessive groundwater extraction and rapid urban development.[[45]](#cite_note-48) Flood control projects and coastal protection measures, including dams and [sea wall systems](/wiki/Giant_Sea_Wall_Jakarta "Giant Sea Wall Jakarta") in the Jakarta Bay, have been developed to mitigate these risks.[[46]](#cite_note-49) [Air pollution](/wiki/Air_pollution "Air pollution") and river pollution are also significant environmental concerns affecting the city.[[47]](#cite_note-50)[[48]](#cite_note-51)[[49]](#cite_note-52)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Rainy_Jakarta.jpg/250px-Rainy_Jakarta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rainy_Jakarta.jpg)

[Drizzle](/wiki/Drizzle "Drizzle") in Jakarta

Jakarta has a [tropical monsoon climate](/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Am*), bordering on a [tropical rainforest climate](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest_climate "Tropical rainforest climate") (Köppen: *Af*). The city experiences a long [wet season](/wiki/Wet_season "Wet season") from October to May and a relatively [drier season](/wiki/Dry_season "Dry season") from June to September, although rainfall occurs throughout the year. The heaviest precipitation typically occurs in January and February, when monthly rainfall averages nearly 300 millimetres, while August is usually the driest month.[[50]](#cite_note-53)

Seasonal weather patterns contribute to recurring flooding in Jakarta. During the wet season, strong monsoon winds and atmospheric circulation over western Java can intensify rainfall and increase river discharge flowing into the city. Combined with Jakarta’s low-lying geography and dense urban development, these conditions can lead to periodic floods and severe thunderstorms.[[51]](#cite_note-54)

Temperatures in Jakarta remain consistently warm throughout the year, with daytime averages typically around 32 °C (89.6 °F) and nighttime temperatures around 24 °C (75.2 °F). Average monthly temperatures vary only slightly, ranging from about 27 °C (80.6 °F) in the coolest months to around 28 °C (82.4 °F) in the warmest months. Recorded extremes range from about 18.9 °C (66.0 °F) to 37.9 °C (100.2 °F).[[52]](#cite_note-55)

## Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/View_of_Jakarta_from_Monas.jpg/1920px-View_of_Jakarta_from_Monas.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_Jakarta_from_Monas.jpg "Panorama of Jakarta as seen from the Monas in a full 360-degree view (river flowing from south-west to north-east, left to right)")

Panorama of Jakarta as seen from the [Monas](/wiki/Monas "Monas") in a full 360-degree view (river flowing from south-west to north-east, left to right)

Jakarta’s cityscape reflects the city’s historical development and its role as Indonesia’s political and economic centre. The urban landscape combines colonial-era buildings, post-independence monumental architecture, modern high-rise developments, and numerous public squares and parks. Much of the capital’s symbolic architecture and urban planning dates from the mid-twentieth century, when then President [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") sought to transform Jakarta into the monumental capital of a newly independent nation.

Several prominent landmarks and monuments were constructed or planned during this period. The most recognisable symbol of the city is the [National Monument](/wiki/National_Monument_(Indonesia) "National Monument (Indonesia)") (*Monas*), a 132-metre-tall (433-foot) obelisk located in the centre of [Merdeka Square](/wiki/Merdeka_Square,_Jakarta "Merdeka Square, Jakarta"). Other notable landmarks include the [Istiqlal mosque](/wiki/Istiqlal_Mosque,_Jakarta "Istiqlal Mosque, Jakarta"), [Jakarta Cathedral](/wiki/Jakarta_Cathedral "Jakarta Cathedral"), and the historic [Batavia Stadhuis](/wiki/Jakarta_History_Museum "Jakarta History Museum") in Jakarta Old Town. Monumental statues and memorials commemorating Indonesian history and national heroes are also prominent features of the city, including the [Tugu Tani monument](/wiki/Heroes_Monument,_Jakarta "Heroes Monument, Jakarta"), the [Dirgantara Monument](/wiki/Dirgantara_Monument "Dirgantara Monument"), and statues honouring figures such as [Diponegoro](/wiki/Diponegoro "Diponegoro") and [Kartini](/wiki/Kartini "Kartini").[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESilver2007101-61)

Jakarta’s modern skyline has continued to evolve with rapid urban development and the construction of high-rise buildings in the central business districts. Structures such as the [Autograph Tower](/wiki/Autograph_Tower "Autograph Tower"), the tallest building in Indonesia, illustrate the city’s contemporary architectural growth. Alongside these developments, Jakarta also contains numerous parks, squares, and public spaces that form important parts of the urban environment and provide recreational areas for residents.

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Architecture")]

See also: [Colonial architecture in Jakarta](/wiki/Colonial_architecture_in_Jakarta "Colonial architecture in Jakarta"), [List of tallest buildings in Jakarta](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Jakarta "List of tallest buildings in Jakarta"), and [Golden Triangle of Jakarta](/wiki/Golden_Triangle_of_Jakarta "Golden Triangle of Jakarta")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Rumah_Betawi_TMII.jpg/250px-Rumah_Betawi_TMII.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rumah_Betawi_TMII.jpg)

[Rumah Kebaya](/wiki/Rumah_Kebaya "Rumah Kebaya") built with [Betawi architecture](/wiki/Betawi_people#Architecture "Betawi people") at [Taman Mini Indonesia Indah](/wiki/Taman_Mini_Indonesia_Indah "Taman Mini Indonesia Indah")

Jakarta contains architecturally significant buildings representing a wide range of historical and cultural influences. Architectural styles in the city reflect Malay, Sundanese, Javanese, Arab, Chinese, and Dutch traditions. Traditional Betawi houses, associated with the indigenous Betawi community, illustrate this mixture of influences. These houses were typically constructed using nangka wood (*[Artocarpus heterophyllus](/wiki/Artocarpus_heterophyllus "Artocarpus heterophyllus")*) and commonly consisted of three main rooms. Their roof forms often resemble the traditional [Javanese joglo](/wiki/Joglo "Joglo"), while features such as wide eaves, large openings, and open layouts were well suited to the tropical climate. The number of officially registered cultural heritage buildings in Jakarta has increased in recent decades as preservation efforts have expanded.[[59]](#cite_note-62)

Many historic structures date from the Dutch colonial era, when Batavia served as the administrative centre of the Dutch East Indies. Colonial architecture can generally be divided into three broad phases: the [Dutch Golden Age](/wiki/Dutch_Golden_Age "Dutch Golden Age") style (17th to late 18th century), a transitional architectural period during the late 18th and 19th centuries, and Dutch modernist architecture of the early 20th century. These buildings include residences, churches, government offices, and commercial structures, many of which remain concentrated in [Jakarta Old Town](/wiki/Jakarta_Old_Town "Jakarta Old Town") and parts of [Central Jakarta](/wiki/Central_Jakarta "Central Jakarta"). Architects such as J.C. Schultze and [Eduard Cuypers](/wiki/Eduard_Cuypers "Eduard Cuypers") designed several notable buildings in the city. Schultze’s works include the [Jakarta Art Building](/wiki/Jakarta_Art_Building "Jakarta Art Building"), the Supreme Court Building, and the Ministry of Finance Building, while Cuypers designed the Bank Indonesia Museum and Mandiri Museum.

During the early 20th century, architectural styles began to shift toward rationalist and modernist influences, including [Art Deco architecture](/wiki/Art_Deco_architecture "Art Deco architecture"). The [Menteng](/wiki/Menteng "Menteng") district, developed in the 1910s as a planned residential suburb, represented one of the earliest attempts to create a modern and healthy residential environment for the middle class. Houses in the area were designed with features suited to the tropical climate, including large windows, open ventilation, and overhanging eaves.[[60]](#cite_note-63)

Post-Indonesian independence architecture in Jakarta reflected the political and cultural priorities of nation-building. Major infrastructure projects were constructed, particularly during the Sukarno era, including the [National Monument](/wiki/National_Monument_(Indonesia) "National Monument (Indonesia)") and the [Senayan Sports Stadium](/wiki/Senayan_Sports_Stadium "Senayan Sports Stadium"), and a network of arterial roads that helped reshape the city’s urban landscape. The [national parliament building](/wiki/MPR/DPR/DPD_building "MPR/DPR/DPD building"), with its distinctive hyperbolic roof, illustrates modernist architectural influences associated with mid-20th century international design. In the 21st century, Jakarta has experienced a rapid urban construction boom, particularly in the [Golden Triangle](/wiki/Golden_Triangle_of_Jakarta "Golden Triangle of Jakarta") [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district"), alongside the rise in skyscrapers.[[61]](#cite_note-64)[[62]](#cite_note-65)

### Parks and public spaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Parks and public spaces")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Smutzer_Primate_Center_Terrace.jpg/250px-Smutzer_Primate_Center_Terrace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Smutzer_Primate_Center_Terrace.jpg)

[Ragunan Zoo Park](/wiki/Ragunan_Zoo_Park "Ragunan Zoo Park") is the largest [urban park](/wiki/Urban_park "Urban park") in Jakarta and the second-largest zoo in the world.

Public parks and green open spaces form an important component of Jakarta’s urban landscape. In June 2011, the city had about 10.5% green open space (*ruang terbuka hijau*, RTH), which had increased to approximately 13.94% in subsequent years as the municipal government expanded park development and environmental programmes. As part of these efforts, the city has created hundreds of child-friendly integrated public spaces (*ruang publik terpadu ramah anak*, RPTRA), with about 300 such facilities operating by 2019.[[63]](#cite_note-66)[[64]](#cite_note-67) In addition to parks, reservoirs and lakes—numbering more than 180 in the wider metropolitan area—also contribute to Jakarta’s environmental infrastructure and flood management system.[[65]](#cite_note-68)

One of the most prominent public spaces in the city is [Merdeka Square](/wiki/Merdeka_Square,_Jakarta "Merdeka Square, Jakarta") (*Medan Merdeka*) in Central Jakarta, a nearly one-square-kilometre open field surrounding the National Monument (Monas). Originally developed in the early nineteenth century under Dutch colonial administration as Koningsplein, the square was later redesigned and beautified, including the addition of landscaped areas and symbolic plantings representing Indonesia’s provinces.[[66]](#cite_note-69) Another important historical square is *[Lapangan Banteng](/wiki/Lapangan_Banteng "Lapangan Banteng")*, located near [Istiqlal Mosque](/wiki/Istiqlal_Mosque,_Jakarta "Istiqlal Mosque, Jakarta") and [Jakarta Cathedral](/wiki/Jakarta_Cathedral "Jakarta Cathedral"). The site served ceremonial functions during the colonial period and now features the [West Irian Liberation Monument](/wiki/West_Irian_Liberation_Monument "West Irian Liberation Monument").[[67]](#cite_note-70)

Jakarta also contains numerous urban parks distributed across the city. [Suropati Park](/wiki/Taman_Suropati "Taman Suropati") in Menteng is known for its landscaped gardens and sculptures created by artists from ASEAN countries,[[68]](#cite_note-71) while nearby [Menteng Park](/wiki/Menteng_Park "Menteng Park") and [Situ Lembang Park](/wiki/Situ_Lembang_Park "Situ Lembang Park") provide recreational spaces in central residential districts. In Jakarta, parks such as [GBK Park](/wiki/GBK_Park "GBK Park"), [Tebet Eco Park](/wiki/Tebet_Eco_Park "Tebet Eco Park"), [Bendera Pusaka Park](/wiki/Bendera_Pusaka_Park "Bendera Pusaka Park"), and [Setu Babakan](/wiki/Setu_Babakan_Betawi_Cultural_Village "Setu Babakan Betawi Cultural Village") serve as important neighbourhood green spaces.[[69]](#cite_note-72) More recent developments include Kalijodo Park in North Jakarta, opened in 2017 with recreational facilities including a skateboard park.[[70]](#cite_note-73)

Several larger recreational and environmental areas are also located within the city. [Ragunan Zoo](/wiki/Ragunan_Zoo "Ragunan Zoo") in South Jakarta is one of the oldest zoos in the world and one of the largest urban parks in the city.[[71]](#cite_note-74) Other natural areas include the [Muara Angke Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Muara_Angke_mangrove_forest_and_wildlife_sanctuary "Muara Angke mangrove forest and wildlife sanctuary") and the [Angke Kapuk Nature Tourism Park](/wiki/Pantai_Indah_Kapuk "Pantai Indah Kapuk") in North Jakarta.[[72]](#cite_note-75) Cultural and recreational complexes such as [Taman Mini Indonesia Indah](/wiki/Taman_Mini_Indonesia_Indah "Taman Mini Indonesia Indah"), which features pavilions representing Indonesia’s provinces, also function as major public attractions and green spaces. [UI Forest](/wiki/UI_Forest "UI Forest") serves as a significant natural reserve and vital ecological area within Jakarta, spanning approximately 100 hectares (250 acres) to provide a sanctuary for over 180 plant species and diverse animal populations.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Jakarta_population_pyramid.svg/250px-Jakarta_population_pyramid.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Jakarta_population_pyramid.svg)

Jakarta population pyramid in 2021

Jakarta has experienced rapid population growth driven largely by migration from across Indonesia. As the country’s political and economic centre, the city attracts residents seeking employment, education, and business opportunities. According to the 1961 census, about 51% of Jakarta’s population had been born in the city,[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECybriwskyFord2001202–13-76) illustrating the long-standing role of migration in shaping its demographic composition.
