With a population of 7.9 million in 1985, Jakarta accounted for 19 per cent of the total Indonesia urban population.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarphamTanner199571-78) Source: [[76]](#cite_note-79) | | |

Population growth accelerated during the second half of the twentieth century.[[77]](#cite_note-80) The population of Jakarta increased from approximately 4.5 million in 1970 to about 9.5 million in 2010,[[78]](#cite_note-81) while the wider Jakarta metropolitan region (Greater Jakarta) expanded from roughly 8.2 million to 28.5 million over the same period. Between 1980 and 1990 the city’s population grew at an average annual rate of about 3.7 percent.

By 2014 the population of Jakarta had reached around 10 million,[[79]](#cite_note-82) with a population density of about 15,174 people per square kilometre.[[80]](#cite_note-83)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHermanto1998?-84) The population of the Greater Jakarta metropolitan area was estimated at approximately 30 million in the same year, representing around 11 percent of Indonesia’s total population.[[82]](#cite_note-85)

Projections suggest that the metropolitan population could reach about 35.6 million by 2030.[[83]](#cite_note-86) Administrative population records compiled by the Ministry of Home Affairs estimated Jakarta’s population at 11,261,595 at the end of 2021. The gender ratio was 102.8 males per 100 females in 2010 and about 101.3 in 2014.[[84]](#cite_note-87)[[85]](#cite_note-88)

### Ethnicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ethnicity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Baju_Demang_Betawi.png/250px-Baju_Demang_Betawi.png)](/wiki/File:Baju_Demang_Betawi.png)

[Betawi](/wiki/Betawi_people "Betawi people") is the native ethnic group in Jakarta

Jakarta is a highly diverse city and does not have a single majority ethnic group. According to the 2010 census, the largest ethnic group in the city was Javanese, accounting for about 36.17% of the population, followed by Betawi (28.29%), Sundanese (14.61%), Chinese Indonesians (6.62%), and Batak (3.42%).[[86]](#cite_note-ETHNICITY-89)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Other communities include Minangkabau, Malays, Madurese, Bugis, Indians, Indo people, and many others from across the Indonesian archipelago.

The [Betawi](/wiki/Betawi_people "Betawi people") (*Orang Betawi*), regarded as the indigenous population of Jakarta, developed from the diverse communities that lived in Batavia during the colonial period. They emerged as a distinct ethnic group by the mid-19th century, largely descended from a mixture of Southeast Asian peoples who were brought to or settled in the city to meet labour needs.[[87]](#cite_note-90) Over time, Betawi culture incorporated influences from many groups, including Chinese, Arab, and European communities.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESáenzEmbrickRodriguez2015?-91) Many Betawi communities historically lived in areas on the fringes of the colonial city, and today Betawi populations are spread throughout the Jakarta metropolitan area.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIyer200123-92) Because of long-standing patterns of migration and intermarriage with other ethnic groups, Betawi identity often reflects a combination of cultural influences within the city.

Jakarta has also long had a significant [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Indonesians "Chinese Indonesians") population. Their communities traditionally lived in older urban districts such as [Glodok](/wiki/Glodok "Glodok"), and parts of [Senen](/wiki/Senen "Senen") and [Jatinegara](/wiki/Jatinegara "Jatinegara"), although today they are distributed more widely throughout the city. Estimates in the early 2000s suggested that Chinese Indonesians accounted for roughly 5–6 percent of Jakarta’s population.[[90]](#cite_note-93) Smaller but historically established communities of [Indians](/wiki/Indian_Indonesians "Indian Indonesians") have also lived in Jakarta for centuries, many of them historically associated with the [Pasar Baru](/wiki/Pasar_Baru "Pasar Baru") district, sometimes referred to as Jakarta’s "Little India."[[91]](#cite_note-94)

Migration from other regions of Indonesia has significantly shaped Jakarta’s modern demographics. Large communities of [Batak](/wiki/Batak "Batak"), [Minangkabau](/wiki/Minangkabau_people "Minangkabau people"), and [Malay](/wiki/Ethnic_Malays "Ethnic Malays") migrants from Sumatra live in the city. According to the 2020 census, Jakarta had approximately 361,000 Batak residents, about 300,960 Minangkabau, and around 101,370 Malays. Toba Batak constitute the largest Batak subgroup in Jakarta,[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReid2010170-95) while Minangkabau migrants have historically been prominent in trade and professional occupations within the city.[[93]](#cite_note-96)[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENaim1971115-131-97)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Language")]

See also: [Betawi language](/wiki/Betawi_language "Betawi language") and [Indonesian slang](/wiki/Indonesian_slang "Indonesian slang")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Bahasa_Betawi.jpg/250px-Bahasa_Betawi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bahasa_Betawi.jpg)

[Betawi language](/wiki/Betawi_language "Betawi language"), a [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") [creole](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language") born in [Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_Dutch_East_Indies "Batavia, Dutch East Indies").

[Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language") is the official and dominant language of Jakarta and is widely used in government, education, business, and everyday communication. [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") is also commonly used, particularly in Central and South Jakarta,[[95]](#cite_note-98) where many international businesses and expatriate communities are located. Many residents of Jakarta continue to use their ethnic languages within families and community settings. The [Betawi language](/wiki/Betawi_language "Betawi language"), associated with the indigenous Betawi community, is a [Malay-based creole](/wiki/Malay_trade_and_creole_languages "Malay trade and creole languages") that developed through centuries of contact among different cultural groups in the city. It incorporates vocabulary from [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"), [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"), [Sundanese](/wiki/Sundanese_language "Sundanese language"), [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language"), [Chinese](/wiki/Hokkien_language "Hokkien language"), and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic"). Over time, many Betawi expressions and phrases have entered colloquial Indonesian and are widely used as part of Jakarta’s urban slang.

[Chinese Indonesians](/wiki/Chinese_Indonesians "Chinese Indonesians") in Jakarta today mainly speak Indonesian and English, although older generations may also speak Chinese dialects such as [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien_dialect "Hokkien dialect") and [other varieties](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") depending on their family background. Migration from Chinese communities in other parts of Indonesia has also introduced dialects such as [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_language "Hakka language"), [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_language "Teochew language"), and [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese_language "Cantonese language") into the city’s Chinese community. Hokkien is commonly spoken among residents with roots in [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra"), particularly in northern districts such as [Pantai Indah Kapuk](/wiki/Pantai_Indah_Kapuk "Pantai Indah Kapuk") and [Pluit](/wiki/Pluit "Pluit"), while Hakka and Teochew are often associated with communities originating from [West Kalimantan](/wiki/West_Kalimantan "West Kalimantan").

Other regional languages of Indonesia are also spoken by migrant communities. Batak residents may speak languages such as Toba Batak, Mandailing, or Karo, while Minangkabau migrants often use the Minangkabau language alongside Indonesian. Sundanese, historically associated with the surrounding region of West Java, is still used by some local communities in Jakarta. It continues to be spoken in several neighbourhoods, particularly in parts of East Jakarta such as Pondok Ranggon and Jatinegara Kaum, where local varieties including Bogor Sundanese[[96]](#cite_note-99) and Banten Sundanese[[97]](#cite_note-100) are maintained by long-established residents.

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [List of universities in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Indonesia "List of universities in Indonesia") and [List of schools in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Indonesia "List of schools in Indonesia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Fakultas_Kedokteran_Universitas_Indonesia.JPG/250px-Fakultas_Kedokteran_Universitas_Indonesia.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fakultas_Kedokteran_Universitas_Indonesia.JPG)

[University of Indonesia](/wiki/University_of_Indonesia "University of Indonesia") (UI) campus, formerly used by [STOVIA](/wiki/STOVIA "STOVIA")

Jakarta is one of Indonesia's principal centres of higher education and hosts numerous universities and research institutions. The [University of Indonesia](/wiki/University_of_Indonesia "University of Indonesia") (UI), the country's largest and one of its oldest universities, maintains campuses in Salemba in Central Jakarta and in nearby [Depok](/wiki/Depok "Depok").[[98]](#cite_note-101) Other public universities located in the city include [Syarif Hidayatullah State Islamic University Jakarta](/wiki/Syarif_Hidayatullah_State_Islamic_University_Jakarta "Syarif Hidayatullah State Islamic University Jakarta") and the [State University of Jakarta](/wiki/State_University_of_Jakarta "State University of Jakarta") (UNJ).[[99]](#cite_note-102)

The city is also home to many private universities and colleges. Major institutions include [Trisakti University](/wiki/Trisakti_University "Trisakti University"), [Atma Jaya Catholic University of Indonesia](/wiki/Atma_Jaya_Catholic_University_of_Indonesia "Atma Jaya Catholic University of Indonesia"), [Bina Nusantara University](/wiki/Bina_Nusantara_University "Bina Nusantara University"), and several others. Vocational higher education institutions, such as [Jakarta State Polytechnic](/wiki/Jakarta_State_Polytechnic "Jakarta State Polytechnic"), also operate in the city. Modern higher education in Jakarta traces its origins to STOVIA (*School tot Opleiding van Indische Artsen*), established in 1851 during the Dutch colonial era to train indigenous physicians.[[100]](#cite_note-103) Today Jakarta attracts students from across Indonesia, many of whom reside in dormitories, rented accommodation, or home-stay residences while pursuing their studies.

For primary and secondary education, Jakarta offers a wide range of public and private schools, including bilingual and international institutions. Several international schools operate in the Jakarta metropolitan area, such as the [Jakarta Intercultural School](/wiki/Jakarta_Intercultural_School "Jakarta Intercultural School"), [Australian Independent School](/wiki/AIS_Indonesia "AIS Indonesia"),[[101]](#cite_note-104) [GMIS Jakarta](/wiki/Gandhi_Memorial_Intercontinental_School "Gandhi Memorial Intercontinental School"), and the [French School Jakarta](/wiki/French_School_Jakarta "French School Jakarta"), among others.

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Religion")]

Religion in Jakarta (2024 Census)[[102]](#cite_note-RELIGION-105)

1. [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") (83.8%)
2. [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") (8.56%)
3. [Roman catholic](/wiki/Roman_catholic "Roman catholic") (3.89%)
4. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (3.46%)
5. [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), and others (0.75%)

Religion plays an important role in Jakarta's social and cultural life. As of 2024, [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Indonesia "Islam in Indonesia") is the dominant religion in the city , followed by [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism_in_Indonesia "Protestantism in Indonesia"), [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Indonesia "Catholic Church in Indonesia"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Indonesia "Buddhism in Indonesia"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Indonesia "Hinduism in Indonesia"), and [Confucianism](/wiki/Supreme_Council_for_the_Confucian_Religion_in_Indonesia "Supreme Council for the Confucian Religion in Indonesia") (1%), with small numbers of residents adhering to other faiths or traditional belief systems.[[102]](#cite_note-RELIGION-105)

Islamic educational and religious institutions are widely present in the city. Many *[pesantren](/wiki/Pesantren "Pesantren")* (Islamic boarding schools) in Jakarta are affiliated with the [traditionalist](/wiki/Traditionalism_(Islam_in_Indonesia) "Traditionalism (Islam in Indonesia)") organisation [Nahdlatul Ulama](/wiki/Nahdlatul_Ulama "Nahdlatul Ulama") (NU),[[103]](#cite_note-106) while [modernist](/wiki/Modernism_(Islam_in_Indonesia) "Modernism (Islam in Indonesia)") Islamic institutions serve urban communities through educational and social programmes.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorter200239-107) Several major Islamic organisations, including the NU, [Muhammadiyah](/wiki/Muhammadiyah "Muhammadiyah"), the [Indonesian Ulema Council](/wiki/Indonesian_Ulema_Council "Indonesian Ulema Council") (MUI), and the [Indonesia Institute of Islamic Dawah](/wiki/Indonesia_Institute_of_Islamic_Dawah "Indonesia Institute of Islamic Dawah"), maintain headquarters in Jakarta.

Christian communities form the second-largest religious group in the city. The Roman Catholic Church is administered by the Archdiocese of Jakarta, which forms part of an ecclesiastical province that also includes West Java and Banten. Protestant denominations are represented by numerous churches throughout the city. Jakarta also has significant Buddhist communities, many of whom are [Chinese Indonesians](/wiki/Chinese_Indonesians "Chinese Indonesians"). Various traditions of Buddhism are practised in the city, including [Theravāda](/wiki/Therav%C4%81da "Theravāda"), [Mahāyāna](/wiki/Mah%C4%81y%C4%81na "Mahāyāna"), and [Tridharma](/wiki/Tridharma "Tridharma"). Smaller religious communities include Hindus—mainly of [Balinese](/wiki/Balinese_people "Balinese people") and [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Indonesians "Indian Indonesians") origin—as well as [Sikh](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") and [Baháʼí](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") communities.[[105]](#cite_note-108)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Category:Companies of Indonesia](/wiki/Category:Companies_of_Indonesia "Category:Companies of Indonesia")

Jakarta GDP share by sector (2022)[[106]](#cite_note-109)

1. Service (75.9%)
2. Manufacturing (12.3%)
3. Other Industrial (11.7%)
4. Agriculture (0.08%)

As the capital and largest city of Indonesia, Jakarta serves as the economic centre of the country and one of the principal financial hubs of Southeast Asia. Indonesia is the largest economy in the [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN"), and Jakarta plays a central role in the national economy. In 2021, the city’s nominal gross regional product was estimated at US$203.7 billion, or about 17% of Indonesia’s gross domestic product, while its purchasing power parity GDP was estimated at US$602.9 billion.[[107]](#cite_note-jakarta2021-110) The city’s nominal GDP per capita was estimated at Rp 274.7 million (US$19,199) in the same year.[[107]](#cite_note-jakarta2021-110)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Jakarta_Scyscrappers_from_GBK.jpg/250px-Jakarta_Scyscrappers_from_GBK.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jakarta_Scyscrappers_from_GBK.jpg)

[SCBD](/wiki/Sudirman_Central_Business_District "Sudirman Central Business District") is a business centre in Jakarta

Jakarta’s economy is dominated by the service sector, particularly finance, trade, and business services, which together account for a large share of the city’s gross regional product.[[27]](#cite_note-Statistics-2008-30) Other important sectors include manufacturing industries such as electronics, automotive production, chemicals, mechanical engineering, and biomedical industries. Finance, ownership and business services form the largest contributor to Jakarta’s economy, followed by trade, hospitality, and manufacturing.[[27]](#cite_note-Statistics-2008-30)

The city hosts the headquarters of many major Indonesian corporations and financial institutions. [Bank Indonesia](/wiki/Bank_Indonesia "Bank Indonesia") and the [Indonesia Stock Exchange](/wiki/Indonesia_Stock_Exchange "Indonesia Stock Exchange") are located in Jakarta, and numerous [state-owned enterprises](/wiki/State-owned_enterprises_of_Indonesia "State-owned enterprises of Indonesia")—including [Pertamina](/wiki/Pertamina "Pertamina"), [PLN](/wiki/Perusahaan_Listrik_Negara "Perusahaan Listrik Negara"), and [Telkomsel](/wiki/Telkomsel "Telkomsel")—maintain their head offices in the city. Large Indonesian conglomerates such as [Salim Group](/wiki/Salim_Group "Salim Group"), [Astra International](/wiki/Astra_International "Astra International"), and [CT Corp](/wiki/CT_Corp "CT Corp") are also headquartered there. Jakarta has also emerged as a centre for technology and digital industries, with global companies such as [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") and [Alibaba](/wiki/Alibaba_Group "Alibaba Group") establishing regional cloud infrastructure in the city.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

Economic growth and investment have contributed to Jakarta’s rapid urban development. In 2017, the city recorded economic growth of 6.22%,[[109]](#cite_note-112) while total investment reached Rp 108.6 trillion (US$8 billion).[[110]](#cite_note-113) Jakarta is also home to several large publicly listed companies and technology start-ups, including firms ranked in the [Forbes Global 2000](/wiki/Forbes_Global_2000 "Forbes Global 2000") and [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500").[[111]](#cite_note-114)[[112]](#cite_note-115)[[113]](#cite_note-116)

Jakarta’s economic landscape also reflects the city’s role as Indonesia’s principal commercial centre. Modern shopping malls and traditional markets form an important part of the urban retail economy, while the tourism sector contributes to municipal revenue through business travel, conventions, and domestic tourism. Rising demand for housing and commercial space has also driven a strong property market,[[114]](#cite_note-117) particularly in central business districts, contributing to increasing land prices and living costs in the city.[[115]](#cite_note-118)

### Shopping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Shopping")]

See also: [List of shopping malls in Jakarta](/wiki/List_of_shopping_malls_in_Jakarta "List of shopping malls in Jakarta")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Inside_of_Grand_Indonesia.jpg/250px-Inside_of_Grand_Indonesia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Inside_of_Grand_Indonesia.jpg)

[Grand Indonesia](/wiki/Grand_Indonesia_Shopping_Town "Grand Indonesia Shopping Town") shopping mall

Jakarta is one of Southeast Asia’s largest retail centres and is known for its extensive network of shopping malls and traditional markets. As of the mid-2010s, the city had one of the largest total [shopping mall floor areas](/wiki/List_of_malls_in_Jakarta "List of malls in Jakarta") among cities worldwide.[[116]](#cite_note-119)[[117]](#cite_note-120) Major shopping centres are concentrated in commercial districts such as Central Jakarta and South Jakarta, where large complexes including [Sarinah](/wiki/Sarinah_Building "Sarinah Building"), [Grand Indonesia](/wiki/Grand_Indonesia "Grand Indonesia") and [Plaza Senayan](/wiki/Plaza_Senayan "Plaza Senayan") serve as prominent retail and entertainment hubs.

In addition to modern malls, Jakarta maintains a long tradition of traditional markets (*pasar*), which continue to play an important role in the city’s retail economy. Well-known markets such as [Tanah Abang](/wiki/Tanah_Abang "Tanah Abang"), [Pasar Baru](/wiki/Pasar_Baru "Pasar Baru"), and [Glodok](/wiki/Glodok "Glodok") sell a wide variety of textiles, clothing, household goods, and food products. Some markets specialise in particular items, including antique goods sold along Jalan Surabaya and gemstones traded at Rawabening Market.[[118]](#cite_note-121)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Further information: [Tourism in Indonesia](/wiki/Tourism_in_Indonesia "Tourism in Indonesia"), [List of museums and cultural institutions in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_museums_and_cultural_institutions_in_Indonesia "List of museums and cultural institutions in Indonesia"), and [Jakarta Tourism and Culture Office](/wiki/Jakarta_Tourism_and_Culture_Office "Jakarta Tourism and Culture Office")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Angel_island_-_The_sixth_stop_%2816932410548%29.jpg/250px-Angel_island_-_The_sixth_stop_%2816932410548%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Angel_island_-_The_sixth_stop_(16932410548).jpg)

*Pulau Bidadari* (Heavenly Nymph island) is one of the [Thousand Islands](/wiki/Thousand_Islands_(Indonesia) "Thousand Islands (Indonesia)") scattered around the sea of Jakarta

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Plaza_Taman_Ismail_Marzuki.jpg/250px-Plaza_Taman_Ismail_Marzuki.jpg)](/wiki/File:Plaza_Taman_Ismail_Marzuki.jpg)

The [Ismail Marzuki Park](/wiki/Ismail_Marzuki_Park "Ismail Marzuki Park") is the most popular [museum](/wiki/Museum "Museum") in Jakarta, there are several [libraries](/wiki/Libraries "Libraries"), [theatres](/wiki/Theatres "Theatres"), and Betawi [performance arts](/wiki/Performance_art "Performance art")

Although Jakarta is not traditionally considered Indonesia’s primary international tourist destination, the city functions as the country’s primary gateway and an important urban tourism centre. Many international visitors pass through Jakarta while travelling to other Indonesian destinations such as [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali"), the [Komodo Island](/wiki/Komodo_Island "Komodo Island"), and [Yogyakarta](/wiki/Yogyakarta "Yogyakarta"). In 2023, approximately 1.97 million foreign tourists visited the city,[[119]](#cite_note-122) while domestic tourism accounts for a large share of overall visitor numbers.

International tourism rankings have identified Jakarta as a rapidly growing destination. According to the [MasterCard Global Destination Cities Index](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_international_visitors "List of cities by international visitors")[[120]](#cite_note-123), the city was among the fastest-growing tourism destinations in the world, while the [World Travel and Tourism Council](/wiki/World_Travel_and_Tourism_Council "World Travel and Tourism Council") has also classified Jakarta as an emerging tourism market with strong projected growth in visitor arrivals.[[121]](#cite_note-124) Euromonitor International’s 2019 ranking placed Jakarta among the 100 most visited cities globally.[[122]](#cite_note-125)

The city government has sought to expand tourism through the development of [meetings, incentives, conferences and exhibitions (MICE) tourism](/wiki/MICE_tourism "MICE tourism"),[[123]](#cite_note-126)[[124]](#cite_note-127) as well as through cultural festivals and large international events. The tourism sector contributes to Jakarta’s economy and municipal revenue, stemming from the city’s role as Indonesia’s principal commercial and transportation hub.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Culture")]

As the capital and largest city of Indonesia, Jakarta is a major centre of cultural exchange and diversity. The city’s population includes people from across the Indonesian archipelago, making its cultural life a mixture of traditions, languages, and customs originating from many regions of the country. Although the [Betawi people](/wiki/Betawi_people "Betawi people") are regarded as Jakarta’s indigenous community, the city’s culture has been shaped by successive waves of migration and interaction among different ethnic groups. [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people") form the largest ethnic group in the capital, followed by Betawi and [Sundanese](/wiki/Sundanese_people "Sundanese people") communities, alongside numerous other groups from throughout Indonesia. This diversity is reflected in Jakarta’s arts, festivals, media, cuisine, and sporting life.

### Arts and festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Arts and festivals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Sang_Pawang_Ondel_Ondel.jpg/250px-Sang_Pawang_Ondel_Ondel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sang_Pawang_Ondel_Ondel.jpg)

*[Ondel-ondel](/wiki/Ondel-ondel "Ondel-ondel")* puppets are the mascot of the city and the symbol of [Betawi](/wiki/Betawi_people "Betawi people") culture.

Jakarta’s artistic and cultural life is closely linked to [Betawi](/wiki/Betawi_people "Betawi people") culture, the traditional culture of the city’s indigenous community. Betawi culture developed through centuries of interaction among Malay, [Sundanese](/wiki/Sundanese_people "Sundanese people"), [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_people "Javanese people"), Chinese, Arab, Indian, and European influences, producing distinctive traditions in language, music, cuisine, and ceremonial practices. Chinese cultural influence is particularly visible in Betawi festivals, wedding traditions, and culinary customs.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Tanjidor%2C_by_M_Jeffry_Hanafiah.jpg/250px-Tanjidor%2C_by_M_Jeffry_Hanafiah.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tanjidor,_by_M_Jeffry_Hanafiah.jpg)

[Tanjidor](/wiki/Tanjidor "Tanjidor") music demonstrates [European](/wiki/Portuguese_Indonesians "Portuguese Indonesians") influence.

Efforts to preserve and promote Betawi arts and traditions are reflected in several community festivals held in different parts of the city,[[125]](#cite_note-128)[[126]](#cite_note-129)[[127]](#cite_note-130) including the *Jalan Jaksa Festival*, *Condet Festival*, and *Lebaran Betawi*. Cultural traditions are also maintained through local celebrations such as the *Sedekah Bumi Keramat Ganceng Festival* in Pondok Ranggon.[[128]](#cite_note-131) These events often feature traditional music, dance performances, and exhibitions of Betawi cultural heritage.

Jakarta has numerous venues dedicated to performing arts and cultural exhibitions. Major cultural centres include the [Taman Ismail Marzuki](/wiki/Taman_Ismail_Marzuki "Taman Ismail Marzuki") arts complex in Cikini, [Gedung Kesenian Jakarta](/wiki/Gedung_Kesenian_Jakarta "Gedung Kesenian Jakarta") near Pasar Baru, and Aula Simfonia Jakarta in Kemayoran. Other venues such as [Balai Sarbini](/wiki/Balai_Sarbini "Balai Sarbini"), [Bentara Budaya Jakarta](/wiki/Bentara_Budaya_Jakarta "Bentara Budaya Jakarta"), and the Pasar Seni art market in [Ancol](/wiki/Ancol "Ancol") host exhibitions, concerts, and theatrical performances. Traditional Indonesian performing arts, including [wayang](/wiki/Wayang "Wayang") puppet theatre and gamelan music, are regularly staged in various venues across the city, including the [Wayang Orang](/wiki/Wayang_wong "Wayang wong") Bharata Theatre and cultural pavilions at [Taman Mini Indonesia Indah](/wiki/Taman_Mini_Indonesia_Indah "Taman Mini Indonesia Indah").

The city also hosts numerous cultural festivals and exhibitions throughout the year. These include film events such as the [Jakarta International Film Festival](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Film_Festival "Jakarta International Film Festival") (JiFFest), as well as large cultural gatherings such as [Jakarta Fashion Week](/wiki/Jakarta_Fashion_Week "Jakarta Fashion Week"), the [Jakarta Fashion & Food Festival](/wiki/Jakarta_Fashion_%26_Food_Festival "Jakarta Fashion & Food Festival"), and the [Jakarta Fair](/wiki/Jakarta_Fair "Jakarta Fair"). Other major events include the [Indonesia International Book Fair](/wiki/Indonesia_International_Book_Fair "Indonesia International Book Fair"), [Indonesia Comic Con](/wiki/Indonesia_Comic_Con "Indonesia Comic Con"), and [Art Jakarta](/wiki/Art_Jakarta "Art Jakarta"), an annual contemporary art fair. The [Jakarta International Java Jazz Festival](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Java_Jazz_Festival "Jakarta International Java Jazz Festival"), held each year in March, is one of the largest jazz festivals in the world.

Jakarta’s cultural landscape is further enriched by the presence of international cultural centres that promote language and the arts. Institutions such as the French [Institut Français d'Indonésie](/wiki/Institut_Fran%C3%A7ais_d%27Indon%C3%A9sie "Institut Français d'Indonésie"), the German [Goethe Institut](/wiki/Goethe_Institut "Goethe Institut"), the [Japan Foundation](/wiki/Japan_Foundation "Japan Foundation"), the [British Council](/wiki/British_Council "British Council") and the Dutch [Erasmus Huis](/wiki/Erasmus_Huis "Erasmus Huis") organise exhibitions, performances, and educational programmes that contribute to the city’s cosmopolitan cultural environment.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Main article: [Betawi cuisine](/wiki/Betawi_cuisine "Betawi cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Soto_Betawi_Jakarta_Street_Side_Food.JPG/250px-Soto_Betawi_Jakarta_Street_Side_Food.JPG)](/wiki/File:Soto_Betawi_Jakarta_Street_Side_Food.JPG)

*[Soto Betawi](/wiki/Soto_Betawi "Soto Betawi")*, mainly consisting of [beef](/wiki/Beef "Beef") or [offal](/wiki/Offal "Offal") in [milk](/wiki/Milk "Milk") or [coconut milk](/wiki/Coconut_milk "Coconut milk") soup.

Jakarta’s culinary culture reflects the city’s long history as a meeting point of diverse ethnic and cultural influences. While [dishes from across Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesian_cuisine "Indonesian cuisine") are widely available in the capital, the traditional local cuisine is [Betawi cuisine](/wiki/Betawi_cuisine "Betawi cuisine"), which developed among the [Betawi people](/wiki/Betawi_people "Betawi people") and incorporates influences from [Malay-Chinese Peranakan cuisine](/wiki/Peranakan_cuisine "Peranakan cuisine"), [Sundanese](/wiki/Sundanese_cuisine "Sundanese cuisine"), [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_cuisine "Javanese cuisine"), Indian, Arab, and European culinary traditions. One of the best-known Betawi dishes is *[Soto](/wiki/Soto_(food)#Varieties "Soto (food)") [Betawi](/wiki/Betawi_people "Betawi people")*, a rich soup made with beef and offal cooked in a spiced broth of coconut milk or cow’s milk. Other traditional dishes include *[ketoprak](/wiki/Ketoprak_(dish) "Ketoprak (dish)")*, *[rujak](/wiki/Rojak#Rujak_Juhi "Rojak")*, and Betawi-style *[gado-gado](/wiki/Gado-gado "Gado-gado")*.

As Indonesia’s largest city, Jakarta is also known for its vibrant [street food](/wiki/Street_food "Street food") culture. [Food travelling vendors](/wiki/Hawker_(trade) "Hawker (trade)") and small roadside eateries (*warung*) serve a wide variety of dishes throughout the city, ranging from common Indonesian foods such as *[nasi goreng](/wiki/Nasi_goreng "Nasi goreng")* (fried rice), *[sate](/wiki/Satay "Satay")* (skewered meats), and *[bakso](/wiki/Bakso "Bakso")* (meatballs) to snacks such as *[bakpau](/wiki/Baozi "Baozi")* (Chinese bun) and *[siomay](/wiki/Siomay "Siomay")* (fish dumplings). Culinary traditions from other Indonesian regions are also strongly represented; for example, [Padang restaurants](/wiki/Padang_cuisine#Padang_restaurants "Padang cuisine") serving [Minangkabau cuisine](/wiki/Minangkabau_cuisine "Minangkabau cuisine") and *[warteg](/wiki/Warung#Varieties "Warung")* (short for *warung tegal*) food stalls offering inexpensive home-style meals are widespread in Jakarta.[[129]](#cite_note-132)

Several districts of the city are known for their concentrations of street food stalls, restaurants, and cafés, including areas such as [Sabang Street](/wiki/Jalan_Jaksa "Jalan Jaksa"), [Blok M](/wiki/Blok_M "Blok M"), and [Kota Tua](/wiki/Kota_Tua_Jakarta "Kota Tua Jakarta").[[130]](#cite_note-133)[[131]](#cite_note-134)[[132]](#cite_note-135) Chinese culinary traditions are especially prominent in parts of West Jakarta, particularly around [Glodok](/wiki/Glodok "Glodok") and [Mangga Besar](/wiki/Mangga_Besar "Mangga Besar"), while the Blok M area has developed a reputation for Japanese restaurants and nightlife venues.[[133]](#cite_note-136) The city government has also established organised food courts such as [Lenggang Jakarta](/wiki/Lenggang_Jakarta "Lenggang Jakarta") and [Thamrin 10](/wiki/Thamrin_10 "Thamrin 10"), which provide spaces for small vendors and street-food traders.[[134]](#cite_note-137)[[135]](#cite_note-138)

Jakarta’s dining scene ranges from modest street vendors to upscale restaurants and international chains.[[136]](#cite_note-139) Local restaurant brands such as [Bakmi GM](/wiki/Bakmi_GM "Bakmi GM"), [D'Cost](/wiki/D%27Cost "D'Cost"), [Es Teler 77](/wiki/Es_Teler_77 "Es Teler 77") and [Sederhana](/wiki/Restoran_Sederhana "Restoran Sederhana") operate alongside global fast-food outlets in shopping centres across the city.[[137]](#cite_note-140) Owing to Jakarta’s cosmopolitan population, restaurants serving international cuisines—including Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Indian, Turkish, Middle Eastern, American, European, and other Asian cuisines—are widely available throughout the capital.

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/U17_World_Cup_Brazil_England.jpg/250px-U17_World_Cup_Brazil_England.jpg)](/wiki/File:U17_World_Cup_Brazil_England.jpg)

[Jakarta International Stadium](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Stadium "Jakarta International Stadium") at [Tanjung Priok](/wiki/Tanjung_Priok "Tanjung Priok"). It is the home of [Persija](/wiki/Persija "Persija") F.C. and has a seating capacity of 82,000. It is Indonesia's biggest stadium.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/GBK_24-10-2018.jpg/250px-GBK_24-10-2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:GBK_24-10-2018.jpg)

[Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium"), home of the Indonesia [Indonesia national football team](/wiki/Indonesia_national_football_team "Indonesia national football team"), has a seating capacity of 78,000. It is Indonesia's second-biggest stadium.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Istora_Senayan_Interior_January_2018.jpg/250px-Istora_Senayan_Interior_January_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Istora_Senayan_Interior_January_2018.jpg)

[Istora Gelora Bung Karno](/wiki/Istora_Gelora_Bung_Karno "Istora Gelora Bung Karno"), home of the [Indonesia national badminton team](/wiki/Indonesia_national_badminton_team "Indonesia national badminton team"), has a capacity of 8,000 seats.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Indonesia_Arena_3_September_2023_-_Pre_Match_BRA_vs_LAT.jpg/250px-Indonesia_Arena_3_September_2023_-_Pre_Match_BRA_vs_LAT.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indonesia_Arena_3_September_2023_-_Pre_Match_BRA_vs_LAT.jpg)

[Indonesia Arena](/wiki/Indonesia_Arena "Indonesia Arena"), is the fields that can be used for [Basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball"), [Volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball"), and [Badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton")

Jakarta has hosted numerous major international sporting events and serves as one of Indonesia’s principal centres for sport. The city hosted the [1962 Asian Games](/wiki/1962_Asian_Games "1962 Asian Games"),[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanna1962193–203-141) and co-hosted the [2018 Asian Games](/wiki/2018_Asian_Games "2018 Asian Games") with [Palembang](/wiki/Palembang "Palembang").[[139]](#cite_note-142) It has also hosted the Southeast Asian Games several times, including in 1979, 1987, 1997, and 2011. Major football tournaments have also taken place in the city; the [Gelora Bung Karno Stadium](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Stadium "Gelora Bung Karno Stadium") was one of the venues for the [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup"), hosting group-stage matches as well as the quarter-final and final.[[140]](#cite_note-143)[[141]](#cite_note-144)[[142]](#cite_note-145) Following the success of the 2018 Asian Games, Jakarta submitted a bid to host the [2032 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2032_Summer_Olympics "2032 Summer Olympics"), although the Games were ultimately awarded to [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane").

The city contains several large sporting venues and complexes. The [Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex](/wiki/Gelora_Bung_Karno_Sports_Complex "Gelora Bung Karno Sports Complex") in [Senayan](/wiki/Senayan "Senayan") is Jakarta’s main sporting hub and includes facilities for football, athletics, aquatic sports, tennis, and indoor arena sports. Other major venues include the [Jakarta International Stadium](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Stadium "Jakarta International Stadium"), a retractable-roof football stadium completed in 2022, the [Jakarta International Velodrome](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Velodrome "Jakarta International Velodrome") in Rawamangun,[[143]](#cite_note-146) and the [Jakarta International Equestrian Park](/wiki/Jakarta_International_Equestrian_Park "Jakarta International Equestrian Park") in Pulomas, both of which were developed or renovated for the 2018 Asian Games.[[144]](#cite_note-147) Indoor arenas such as the [BritAma Arena](/wiki/BritAma_Arena "BritAma Arena") and facilities in [Kelapa Gading](/wiki/Kelapa_Gading "Kelapa Gading") are also used for national basketball competitions and other sporting events.

Football is the most popular sport in Jakarta. The city’s main professional club is [Persija](/wiki/Persija_Jakarta "Persija Jakarta"), which competes in Indonesia’s top football league, [Liga 1](/wiki/Liga_1_(Indonesia) "Liga 1 (Indonesia)"), while [Persitara](/wiki/Persitara_Jakarta_Utara "Persitara Jakarta Utara") plays in the lower-tier [Liga 3](/wiki/Liga_3_(Indonesia) "Liga 3 (Indonesia)"). Jakarta also hosts large-scale sporting events such as the [Jakarta Marathon](/wiki/Jakarta_Marathon "Jakarta Marathon"), established in 2013 and recognised by international athletics organisations, attracting thousands of participants from many countries each year.[[145]](#cite_note-148)[[146]](#cite_note-149)

In addition to professional sports, recreational and community sporting activities are common in the city. One well-known example is the [Jakarta Car-Free Day](/wiki/Car-free_days "Car-free days"), held regularly on major central avenues such as Jalan Sudirman and Jalan Thamrin, when sections of the roads are closed to motor vehicles. During these events, residents participate in activities such as jogging, cycling, aerobics, and other outdoor sports.[[147]](#cite_note-150) Jakarta has also hosted international motorsport events, including the [Formula E](/wiki/Formula_E "Formula E") [Jakarta ePrix](/wiki/Jakarta_ePrix "Jakarta ePrix"), first held in 2022 at the [Ancol Circuit](/wiki/Jakarta_International_e-Prix_Circuit "Jakarta International e-Prix Circuit"), North Jakarta.[[148]](#cite_note-151)

### Media and entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Media and entertainment")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/TVRITower.jpg/250px-TVRITower.jpg)](/wiki/File:TVRITower.jpg)

The main [TV tower](/wiki/TVRI_Tower "TVRI Tower") of [TVRI](/wiki/TVRI "TVRI") at its headquarters in Jakarta

See also: [List of newspapers in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_Indonesia "List of newspapers in Indonesia"), [List of radio stations in Jakarta](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Jakarta "List of radio stations in Jakarta"), and [List of television stations in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_television_stations_in_Indonesia "List of television stations in Indonesia")

Jakarta is the centre of Indonesia’s national media industry and hosts most of the country’s major newspapers, broadcasters, and publishing companies. Several national newspapers are based in the city, including *[Kompas](/wiki/Kompas "Kompas")* and *[Media Indonesia](/wiki/Media_Indonesia "Media Indonesia")*, while local newspapers such as *[Pos Kota](/wiki/Pos_Kota "Pos Kota")* and *[Warta Kota](/wiki/Warta_Kota "Warta Kota")* focus primarily on news and events within the capital. A number of specialised publications are also produced in Jakarta, including business newspapers such as *[Bisnis Indonesia](/wiki/Bisnis_Indonesia "Bisnis Indonesia")* and *[Investor Daily](/wiki/Investor_Daily "Investor Daily")*, as well as the sports newspaper *[Super Ball](/wiki/Tribun_Network "Tribun Network")*.

The city is also home to several newspapers published in foreign languages for national and international audiences. English-language publications include *[The Jakarta Post](/wiki/The_Jakarta_Post "The Jakarta Post")* and the online news outlet *[The Jakarta Globe](/wiki/The_Jakarta_Globe "The Jakarta Globe")*. Chinese-language newspapers such as *[Harian Indonesia](/wiki/Harian_Indonesia "Harian Indonesia")* (印尼星洲日报), and *[Guoji Ribao](/wiki/International_Daily_News "International Daily News")* (国际日报) circulate among the Indonesian Chinese community, while the Japanese-language newspaper *[The Daily Jakarta Shimbun](/wiki/The_Daily_Jakarta_Shimbun "The Daily Jakarta Shimbun")* (じゃかるた新聞) serves Japanese readers in Indonesia.

Radio broadcasting remains an important part of Jakarta’s media landscape. Dozens of stations operate in the metropolitan area, including both public and private networks. National radio networks based in Jakarta include [Prambors FM](/wiki/Prambors_FM "Prambors FM"), [Hard Rock FM](/wiki/Hard_Rock_FM "Hard Rock FM"), and Delta FM, while public broadcasting services are provided by *[Radio Republik Indonesia](/wiki/Radio_Republik_Indonesia "Radio Republik Indonesia")* (RRI). Local stations such as *[Gen FM](/wiki/Gen_FM "Gen FM")* and *[Radio Elshinta](/wiki/Radio_Elshinta "Radio Elshinta")* also broadcast from the city.

Jakarta is likewise the headquarters of Indonesia’s public television broadcaster [TVRI](/wiki/TVRI "TVRI") and several private national television networks, including [Metro TV](/wiki/Metro_TV_(Indonesian_TV_network) "Metro TV (Indonesian TV network)"), [tvOne](/wiki/TvOne_(Indonesian_TV_network) "TvOne (Indonesian TV network)"), [Kompas TV](/wiki/Kompas_TV "Kompas TV"), and [RCTI](/wiki/RCTI "RCTI"). The city also has local television stations such as [TVRI Jakarta](/wiki/TVRI_Jakarta "TVRI Jakarta"), [JakTV](/wiki/JakTV "JakTV"), and Elshinta TV. In recent years, Indonesia has begun transitioning from analogue broadcasting to digital television using the DVB-T2 standard as part of a [nationwide digital migration programme](/wiki/Digital_terrestrial_television_in_Indonesia "Digital terrestrial television in Indonesia").[[149]](#cite_note-152)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main article: [Governor of Jakarta](/wiki/Governor_of_Jakarta "Governor of Jakarta")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Balaikota_DKI_Jakarta.jpg/250px-Balaikota_DKI_Jakarta.jpg)](/wiki/File:Balaikota_DKI_Jakarta.jpg)

[Jakarta City Hall](/wiki/Jakarta_City_Hall "Jakarta City Hall")

Jakarta has a special administrative status equivalent to that of a [province](/wiki/Provinces_of_Indonesia "Provinces of Indonesia") within Indonesia. The provincial government is led by a directly elected [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Jakarta "Governor of Jakarta") and [vice governor](/wiki/Vice_Governor_of_Jakarta "Vice Governor of Jakarta"), while legislative authority is exercised by the [Jakarta Regional People's Representative Council](/wiki/Jakarta_Regional_People%27s_Representative_Council "Jakarta Regional People's Representative Council") (*Dewan Perwakilan Rakyat Daerah*, DPRD DKI Jakarta), whose members are elected through provincial elections. The governor’s office and main administrative headquarters are located at the [Jakarta City Hall](/wiki/Jakarta_City_Hall "Jakarta City Hall"), south of [Merdeka Square](/wiki/Merdeka_Square,_Jakarta "Merdeka Square, Jakarta").

The provincial administration oversees five administrative cities (*kota administrasi*)—Central Jakarta, West Jakarta, South Jakarta, East Jakarta, and North Jakarta—and one [administrative regency](/wiki/Regencies_of_Indonesia "Regencies of Indonesia"), the Thousand Islands (*Kepulauan Seribu*). Each administrative city is headed by a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") (*walikota*) and the regency by a [regent](/wiki/Regent "Regent") (*bupati*), all of whom are appointed by the governor rather than directly elected. These administrative units are further divided into districts (*kecamatan*).

At the national level, Jakarta is represented in Indonesia’s national legislature. The province elects 21 members to the [People's Representative Council](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_(Indonesia) "House of Representatives (Indonesia)") (*Dewan Perwakilan Rakyat*, DPR) from three electoral districts. Jakarta I corresponds to East Jakarta, Jakarta II includes Central and South Jakarta together with Indonesian voters residing overseas,[[150]](#cite_note-153) and Jakarta III comprises North and West Jakarta as well as the Thousand Islands. Like all Indonesian provinces, Jakarta also sends four representatives to the [Regional Representative Council](/wiki/Regional_Representative_Council "Regional Representative Council") (*Dewan Perwakilan Daerah*, DPD), the upper chamber of parliament.

The provincial government has also promoted digital governance initiatives. One example is the Jakarta Smart City (JSC) programme, launched in 2014 to improve urban management through data integration, online services, and mobile applications aimed at enhancing public services, mobility, environmental monitoring, and civic participation.[[151]](#cite_note-154)

### Public safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Public safety")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Polda_Metro_Jaya_dari_Simpang_Susun_Semanggi_%282024%29.jpg/250px-Polda_Metro_Jaya_dari_Simpang_Susun_Semanggi_%282024%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Polda_Metro_Jaya_dari_Simpang_Susun_Semanggi_(2024).jpg)

The Headquarters of [Polda Metro Jaya](/wiki/Greater_Jakarta_Metropolitan_Regional_Police "Greater Jakarta Metropolitan Regional Police")

Public safety in Jakarta is primarily the responsibility of the [Greater Jakarta Metropolitan Regional Police](/wiki/Greater_Jakarta_Metropolitan_Regional_Police "Greater Jakarta Metropolitan Regional Police") (*Polda Metro Jaya*), which oversees law enforcement across the Jakarta metropolitan area. It is led by a [two-star police general](/wiki/Inspector_General_of_Police#Indonesia "Inspector General of Police") (*Kapolda Metro Jaya*). The [Indonesian Army](/wiki/Indonesian_Army "Indonesian Army"), led by a Major General (*Pangdam Jaya*), also maintains a regional command in the city, the [Jayakarta Military Regional Command](/wiki/Kodam_Jaya "Kodam Jaya") (*Kodam Jaya*), which functions as part of the national defence structure and supports security operations in coordination with civilian authorities when required.

### Municipal finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Municipal finances")]

Jakarta’s provincial government relies heavily on transfers from the national government for much of its revenue. Locally generated income is derived mainly from taxes, including vehicle ownership and vehicle transfer fees.[[152]](#cite_note-155) Despite significant fiscal resources, the provincial administration often records budget surpluses—typically between 15 and 20 percent of planned spending—largely due to delays in procurement and other administrative inefficiencies.[[153]](#cite_note-156)[[154]](#cite_note-157)

Public expenditure has prioritised sectors such as education, transportation infrastructure, flood control, environmental management, and social services including healthcare and housing.[[155]](#cite_note-158) Jakarta’s regional budget (*Anggaran Pendapatan dan Belanja Daerah*, APBD) has grown steadily in recent years, reaching approximately Rp77.1 trillion in 2017, Rp83.2 trillion in 2018, and Rp89 trillion in 2019.[[156]](#cite_note-159)[[157]](#cite_note-160)[[158]](#cite_note-161)

### Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

See also: [List of districts of Jakarta](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Jakarta "List of districts of Jakarta")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Special_Capital_Region_of_Jakarta.png/250px-Special_Capital_Region_of_Jakarta.png)](/wiki/File:Special_Capital_Region_of_Jakarta.png)

Map of the administrative cities (*Kota administratif*) in Jakarta province; the Thousand Islands Regency (which is to the north) is shown in the inset to the lower left.

Jakarta is divided into five administrative cities (*Kota Administratif*) and one [administrative regency](/wiki/Regencies_of_Indonesia "Regencies of Indonesia") (*Kabupaten Administratif*), each headed by a mayor or regent. Unlike other Indonesian municipalities, these administrative divisions do not have their own local legislatures and remain directly subordinate to the provincial government of Jakarta. Each administrative city is further divided into [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Indonesia "Districts of Indonesia") (*[Kecamatan](/wiki/Kecamatan "Kecamatan")*).

The five administrative cities are [Central Jakarta](/wiki/Central_Jakarta "Central Jakarta"), [West Jakarta](/wiki/West_Jakarta "West Jakarta"), [South Jakarta](/wiki/South_Jakarta "South Jakarta"), [East Jakarta](/wiki/East_Jakarta "East Jakarta"), and [North Jakarta](/wiki/North_Jakarta "North Jakarta"), while the [Thousand Islands](/wiki/Thousand_Islands_(Indonesia) "Thousand Islands (Indonesia)") (*Kepulauan Seribu*) form the province’s sole administrative regency.[[159]](#cite_note-162) Central Jakarta is the smallest of the municipalities and serves as the political and administrative centre of the capital, containing major national landmarks such as the [National Monument (*Monas*)](/wiki/National_Monument_(Indonesia) "National Monument (Indonesia)"), [Istiqlal Mosque](/wiki/Istiqlal_Mosque,_Jakarta "Istiqlal Mosque, Jakarta"), [Jakarta Cathedral](/wiki/Jakarta_Cathedral "Jakarta Cathedral"), and several national museums.[[160]](#cite_note-163)

West Jakarta has a high concentration of small-scale industries and contains parts of [Jakarta Old Town](/wiki/Jakarta_Old_Town "Jakarta Old Town"),[[161]](#cite_note-164) including Dutch colonial era buildings such as *[Toko Merah](/wiki/Toko_Merah "Toko Merah")* and structures displaying Chinese architectural influence. South Jakarta, originally planned as a satellite city, has developed into an important commercial and affluent residential area, hosting major shopping centres and parts of Jakarta’s [central business district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district"), including the [Golden Triangle](/wiki/Golden_Triangle_of_Jakarta "Golden Triangle of Jakarta") around [Kebayoran Baru](/wiki/Kebayoran_Baru "Kebayoran Baru") and [Setiabudi](/wiki/Setiabudi "Setiabudi").[[162]](#cite_note-165)

East Jakarta is characterised by large industrial zones[[163]](#cite_note-166) and several major facilities, including [Taman Mini Indonesia Indah](/wiki/Taman_Mini_Indonesia_Indah "Taman Mini Indonesia Indah") and [Halim Perdanakusuma Airport](/wiki/Halim_Perdanakusuma_Airport "Halim Perdanakusuma Airport"). North Jakarta borders the Java Sea and contains the [Port of Tanjung Priok](/wiki/Port_of_Tanjung_Priok "Port of Tanjung Priok"), Indonesia’s busiest seaport, as well as the [Ancol Dreamland](/wiki/Ancol_Dreamland "Ancol Dreamland") tourism complex.[[164]](#cite_note-167) The Thousand Islands regency consists of more than one hundred small islands in the Java Sea and is known for its marine ecosystems and tourism activities such as diving and island recreation.

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

Jakarta has undertaken major infrastructure development projects aimed at improving urban livability and addressing long-standing challenges such as traffic congestion, flooding, and inadequate public services. One large-scale initiative is a ten-year urban regeneration programme valued at approximately Rp571 trillion (US$40.5 billion), which focuses on expanding public transportation, improving clean water and wastewater systems, upgrading housing, and strengthening flood-control infrastructure.[[169]](#cite_note-172)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Main article: [Transport in Jakarta](/wiki/Transport_in_Jakarta "Transport in Jakarta")

[![KRL](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/JR_205_Keiyo_12sf_%40_Tebet-Cawang_%2820230713%29_%2853328327787%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-JR_205_Keiyo_12sf_%40_Tebet-Cawang_%2820230713%29_%2853328327787%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:JR_205_Keiyo_12sf_@_Tebet-Cawang_(20230713)_(53328327787)_(cropped).jpg)

[![MRT](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/MRTset12_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-MRTset12_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:MRTset12_(cropped).jpg)

[![LRT](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/LRT_Di_Jatimulya_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-LRT_Di_Jatimulya_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:LRT_Di_Jatimulya_(cropped).jpg)

[![Airport rail link](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Jakarta_Airport_Train_EA203_%40_Grogol_%2820230714%29_%2853330065885%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jakarta_Airport_Train_EA203_%40_Grogol_%2820230714%29_%2853330065885%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jakarta_Airport_Train_EA203_@_Grogol_(20230714)_(53330065885)_(cropped).jpg)

[![LRT](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/LRT_Jakarta_-_Hyundai_Rotem_LRV_in_Boulevard_Utara_Station.jpg/250px-LRT_Jakarta_-_Hyundai_Rotem_LRV_in_Boulevard_Utara_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:LRT_Jakarta_-_Hyundai_Rotem_LRV_in_Boulevard_Utara_Station.jpg)

[![BRT](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/SJM-240014_Sinar_Jaya_Transjakarta_E-Cityline_3_Laksana.jpg/250px-SJM-240014_Sinar_Jaya_Transjakarta_E-Cityline_3_Laksana.jpg)](/wiki/File:SJM-240014_Sinar_Jaya_Transjakarta_E-Cityline_3_Laksana.jpg)

Various means of public transport in Jakarta; from top up to down: [KRL Commuterline](/wiki/KRL_Commuterline "KRL Commuterline"), [Jakarta MRT](/wiki/Jakarta_MRT "Jakarta MRT"), [Jabodebek LRT](/wiki/Jabodebek_LRT "Jabodebek LRT"), [Soekarno–Hatta Airport Rail Link](/wiki/Soekarno%E2%80%93Hatta_Airport_Rail_Link "Soekarno–Hatta Airport Rail Link"), [Jakarta LRT](/wiki/Jakarta_LRT "Jakarta LRT"), and [Transjakarta](/wiki/Transjakarta "Transjakarta")

Transportation in Jakarta reflects the scale and density of the Greater [Jakarta metropolitan area](/wiki/Jakarta_metropolitan_area "Jakarta metropolitan area"), which has a population of more than 40 million.[[170]](#cite_note-UNpopulation-173) Although the city historically prioritized road development to accommodate private vehicles,[[171]](#cite_note-Easts.info-174) recent policies have increasingly focused on expanding public transportation and improving mobility. Jakarta received the 2021 Global [Sustainable Transport Award](/wiki/Sustainable_Transport_Award "Sustainable Transport Award") in recognition of efforts to integrate its public transit system,[[172]](#cite_note-175) though transport infrastructure continues to grapple with keeping pace with population growth.[[173]](#cite_note-NYT8413-176)[[174]](#cite_note-177)

Road transport remains dominant in the city. Jakarta’s road system includes an extensive toll road network consisting of [inner](/wiki/Jakarta_Inner_Ring_Road "Jakarta Inner Ring Road") and [outer ring roads](/wiki/Jakarta_Outer_Ring_Road "Jakarta Outer Ring Road") and several radial expressways connecting the city with surrounding regions. Traffic congestion has long remained a significant problem,[[175]](#cite_note-178) and policies such as the "odd–even" vehicle restriction system have been introduced to limit road use by private cars on certain days as a transitional measure before the planned implementation of electronic [road pricing](/wiki/Road_pricing "Road pricing").

Public transportation has expanded significantly since the 2010s and now includes several mass transit systems serving the wider [metropolitan area](/wiki/Jakarta_metropolitan_area "Jakarta metropolitan area"). These include the [TransJakarta](/wiki/TransJakarta "TransJakarta") bus rapid transit system, the [Jakarta MRT](/wiki/Jakarta_MRT "Jakarta MRT") and [LRT](/wiki/Jakarta_LRT "Jakarta LRT"), the [KRL Commuterline](/wiki/KRL_Commuterline "KRL Commuterline") suburban railway, and the [Soekarno–Hatta Airport Rail Link](/wiki/Soekarno%E2%80%93Hatta_Airport_Rail_Link "Soekarno–Hatta Airport Rail Link"). Transit-oriented developments such as [Dukuh Atas](/wiki/Dukuh_Atas_TOD "Dukuh Atas TOD") and [CSW–ASEAN](/wiki/CSW-ASEAN_TOD "CSW-ASEAN TOD") have been built to improve integration between different transport modes. By 2023, public transport services covered approximately 86% of the city, with a target of reaching 95%.

Jakarta is served by several major transportation hubs. [Soekarno–Hatta International Airport](/wiki/Soekarno%E2%80%93Hatta_International_Airport "Soekarno–Hatta International Airport") is the main gateway for air travel to the Greater Jakarta region, while [Halim Perdanakusuma Airport](/wiki/Halim_Perdanakusuma_Airport "Halim Perdanakusuma Airport") handles limited domestic and private flights. The city’s principal seaport is [Tanjung Priok](/wiki/Tanjung_Priok "Tanjung Priok"), Indonesia’s busiest port, which connects Jakarta with other parts of the archipelago. Smaller ports such as Sunda Kelapa and Muara Angke[[176]](#cite_note-179) support traditional shipping and ferry services, including connections to the [Thousand Islands](/wiki/Thousand_Islands_(Indonesia) "Thousand Islands (Indonesia)").

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

Jakarta has an extensive [healthcare system](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Indonesia "Healthcare in Indonesia") consisting of both public and private facilities. In 2012, then Jakarta Governor [Joko Widodo](/wiki/Joko_Widodo "Joko Widodo") introduced the 'Healthy Jakarta Card' (*Kartu Jakarta Sehat*, KJS),[[177]](#cite_note-180) a regional universal healthcare programme aimed at expanding access to medical services for residents. In January 2014, the Indonesian government launched a nationwide universal healthcare system known as the 'National Health Insurance' (*[Jaminan Kesehatan Nasional](/wiki/Jaminan_Kesehatan_Nasional "Jaminan Kesehatan Nasional"), JKN)*, administered by the [Social Security Agency on Health](/wiki/BPJS_Kesehatan "BPJS Kesehatan") (*BPJS Kesehatan*).[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBritnell201547-181) The Jakarta programme was later integrated into the national scheme,[[179]](#cite_note-182) and KJS cards remained valid until 2018.[[180]](#cite_note-183) As of 2021, approximately 85.55% of Jakarta's population was covered by the JKN programme.[[181]](#cite_note-184)

Public healthcare facilities in the city include major government-run hospitals such as the [Dr. Cipto Mangunkusumo Hospital](/wiki/Dr._Cipto_Mangunkusumo_Hospital "Dr. Cipto Mangunkusumo Hospital"), [Gatot Soebroto Army Hospital](/wiki/Gatot_Soebroto_Army_Hospital "Gatot Soebroto Army Hospital"), as well as community hospitals and local public health centres (*[puskesmas](/wiki/Puskesmas "Puskesmas")*). While public hospitals generally provide a good standard of care, they are often overcrowded due to high demand. Private hospitals and clinics also play an important role in Jakarta’s healthcare system. The sector has expanded since 2010, when the Indonesian government allowed foreign investment in private healthcare services. Some facilities are operated by nonprofit or religious organisations, while many others are run by private hospital groups such as [Siloam](/wiki/Siloam_Hospitals "Siloam Hospitals"), Pondok Indah Hospital Group, and Mitra Keluarga.[[182]](#cite_note-185)[[183]](#cite_note-186)[[184]](#cite_note-187)

### Water supply

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Water supply")]

Further information: [Water privatisation in Jakarta](/wiki/Water_privatisation_in_Jakarta "Water privatisation in Jakarta")

Jakarta’s piped water supply is operated through a concession system involving private companies and a public asset holder. Since 1998, two private operators—PALYJA and Aetra—have provided piped water services in the western and eastern parts of the city respectively under concession agreements, while the infrastructure is owned by the public company PAM Jaya. Much of the city’s water supply originates from the West Tarum Canal system, which carries water from the Jatiluhur reservoir on the [Citarum River](/wiki/Citarum_River "Citarum River"), about 70 km (43 mi) southeast of Jakarta. The water supply system was originally privatised in 1998, when concessions were granted to the French company [Suez Environnement](/wiki/Suez_Environnement "Suez Environnement") and the British company [Thames Water](/wiki/Thames_Water "Thames Water"). These companies later transferred their stakes to Indonesian operators. During the early years of the concession system, customer growth was slower than expected, partly due to tariff increases adjusted for inflation. Tariffs were later frozen in 2005, which reduced investment by private operators.

Access to piped water has improved over time but remains uneven across the city. According to PALYJA data, service coverage in western Jakarta increased from about 34% in 1998 to 65% in 2010,[[185]](#cite_note-188) while access in eastern Jakarta rose from roughly 57% in 1998 to around 67% in 2004 before stagnating.[[d]](#cite_note-189)[[186]](#cite_note-190) However, some independent studies have estimated much lower levels of direct household access, with figures as low as 25% in 2005 and 18.5% in 2011.[[187]](#cite_note-191)[[188]](#cite_note-192) Many residents without piped water rely on groundwater wells, which are often saline or contaminated. As of 2017, the [Ministry of Energy and Mineral Resources](/wiki/Ministry_of_Energy_and_Mineral_Resources_(Indonesia) "Ministry of Energy and Mineral Resources (Indonesia)") reported that Jakarta was facing a significant clean water crisis.[[189]](#cite_note-193)

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: International relations")]

See also: [List of diplomatic missions in Jakarta](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_in_Jakarta "List of diplomatic missions in Jakarta")

Jakarta's role as Indonesia’s capital and largest city extends far beyond domestic governance, positioning the city as a primary geopolitical and diplomatic hub within the Indo-Pacific region. By hosting a vast concentration of [foreign embassies](/wiki/Diplomatic_mission "Diplomatic mission"), the city serves as the essential point of contact for international relations, while its status as the permanent headquarters for the [Association of Southeast Asian Nations](/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Nations "Association of Southeast Asian Nations") (ASEAN) cements its reputation as the "diplomatic capital of Southeast Asia."[[190]](#cite_note-194) This institutional presence grants Jakarta significant influence over regional policy-making and economic cooperation.

Furthermore, the city’s global footprint is expanded through its active membership in specialized international networks that address modern urban challenges. Participation in the [C40 Cities Climate Leadership Group](/wiki/C40_Cities_Climate_Leadership_Group "C40 Cities Climate Leadership Group") demonstrates a commitment to global environmental standards and climate resilience, while involvement in the [Asian Network of Major Cities 21](/wiki/Asian_Network_of_Major_Cities_21 "Asian Network of Major Cities 21") and the [ASEAN Smart Cities Network](/wiki/ASEAN_Smart_Cities_Network "ASEAN Smart Cities Network") facilitates the exchange of technological innovation and infrastructure strategies. Collectively, these affiliations transform Jakarta from a local metropolis into a collaborative player on the world stage, deeply integrated into global efforts regarding sustainability, urban development, and regional security.

### Twin towns – sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jakarta&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Twin towns – sister cities")]

See also: [List of twin towns and sister cities in Indonesia](/wiki/List_of_twin_towns_and_sister_cities_in_Indonesia "List of twin towns and sister cities in Indonesia")

Jakarta signed [sister city](/wiki/Sister_city "Sister city") agreements with other cities, including [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca"). To promote friendship between the two cities, the main avenue famous for its shopping and business centres was named after Jakarta's Moroccan sister city. No street in [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca") is named after Jakarta. However, the Moroccan capital city of [Rabat](/wiki/Rabat "Rabat") has an avenue named after [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno"), Indonesia's first president, to commemorate his visit in 1960 and as a token of friendship.[[191]](#cite_note-JakartaPost2-195)

Jakarta's sister cities are:[[192]](#cite_note-sisters-196)

* [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), Thailand
* [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), China
* [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), Germany
* [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca"), Morocco
* [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"), Palestine
* [Hanoi](/wiki/Hanoi "Hanoi"), Vietnam
* [Islamabad](/wiki/Islamabad "Islamabad"), Pakistan
* [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), Turkey
* [Jeddah](/wiki/Jeddah "Jeddah"), Saudi Arabia
* [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), Ukraine
* [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), United States
* [Maputo](/wiki/Maputo "Maputo"), Mozambique
* [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), India[[193]](#cite_note-197)
* [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), Russia
* [Pyongyang](/wiki/Pyongyang "Pyongyang"), North Korea
* [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), South Korea
* [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China
* [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan

Jakarta also has established a partnership with [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), especially on integrated urban water management, including capacity-building and knowledge exchange.[[194]](#cite_note-NESO-198) This cooperation is mainly because both cities are dealing with similar problems; they lie in low-lying flat plains and are prone to flooding. Additionally, they have both implemented drainage systems involving canals, dams and pumps vital for both cities for below-sea-level areas.

In addition to its sister cities, Jakarta cooperates with:[[192]](#cite_note-sisters-196)

* [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"), United States
* [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), Hungary
* [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), Australia
* [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), France
* [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam"), Netherlands
